name: CoStar Group, Inc.

profile.description: CoStar Group, Inc. provides information, analytics, and online marketplaces for the commercial and residential real estate industries. Products include CoStar (commercial data platform), Apartments.com (multifamily listings), Homes.com (residential marketplace), LoopNet (commercial listings), STR (hospitality benchmarking), Ten-X (online auction), and Matterport (3D digital twin technology). Founded in 1986 by Andy Florance, headquartered in Arlington, Virginia. Member of the S&P 500 and NASDAQ 100.

innovation.label: STRONG
innovation.analysis: Track Record (3/3): CoStar has executed multiple transformative pivots over 40 years — from commercial real estate data provider to online marketplace platform, adding Apartments.com ($585M acquisition in 2014), launching Homes.com to challenge Zillow in residential, and acquiring Matterport for 3D digital twin technology [1][2]. Each move expanded the company's addressable market by orders of magnitude. AI Deployment (2/2): Homes.com launched a transformative AI-powered search application in February 2026 that replaced traditional filter-based property search with dynamic AI-driven results, driving a 119% increase in organic traffic in Q1 2026 [1][2]. This is a shipping AI product with measurable engagement impact. Market Share (1/2): CoStar dominates commercial real estate information with an effective monopoly in CRE data (which is now the subject of antitrust litigation), and Apartments.com leads multifamily listings [3]. However, Homes.com remains a distant challenger to Zillow in residential. Product Pipeline (1/2): Matterport 3D digital twin integration across CoStar platforms, continued Homes.com AI enhancements, and STR hospitality analytics expansion provide visible pipeline, though specific revenue targets are limited [2]. R&D Intensity (1/1): CoStar invests heavily in technology with over 2,000 engineers, and R&D spending significantly exceeds real estate services industry norms.

inspiration.label: STRONG
inspiration.analysis: Mission Floor (3/4): Andy Florance founded CoStar in 1986 at Princeton with the specific vision of digitizing commercial real estate information to create market transparency — a purpose that has been validated by nearly 40 years of consistent execution [2]. The mission to 'democratize real estate information' has guided every major strategic decision from CoStar's founding through Apartments.com and Homes.com. This is genuinely distinctive and has shaped competitive advantage. Community Impact (0/2): No evidence of distinctive charitable programs or community investment beyond standard corporate practices. Employee Retention (2/2): Andy Florance has led CoStar for nearly 40 years as founder-CEO, providing exceptional leadership continuity [2]. Named top 10 on the Swanepoel Power 200 in 2026. The company has been recognized by Forbes and Fortune among the most innovative companies. Senior leadership shows long tenure aligned with Florance's vision. Insider Buying (2/2): CEO Andy Florance purchased approximately 71,000 shares ($2.5M) on the open market in May 2026 at ~$35.20/share, with net insider buying of $5.0M over the past 12 months [5]. This represents meaningful personal conviction from the founder-CEO.

infrastructure.label: STRONG
infrastructure.analysis: Mechanically scored on available metrics. CoStar carries virtually no debt with D/E of just 0.15%, providing maximum financial flexibility [4]. Revenue grew 23% YoY to $897M in Q1 2026 with full-year guidance of $3.78-3.82B (17% growth) [1]. Forward P/E of 20.83 is near the 20 target, and PEG of 0.54 (2x weight) indicates the growth rate justifies the valuation. The primary weakness is current profitability: GAAP net income of just $3M in Q1 2026 (0.73% profit margin) and ROE of 0.30% reflect heavy investment in Homes.com growth (marketing spend) compressing near-term earnings [1]. Adjusted EBITDA of $132M (up 100% YoY) demonstrates underlying profitability improving rapidly. Five-year revenue CAGR of approximately 14% approaches the 10% target.

ai_resilience.label: MODERATE
ai_resilience.analysis: AI Risk Protection (6/9): CoStar's core commercial real estate data business is protected by decades of proprietary data collection, strong switching costs, and a network effect that makes its CRE information platform difficult to replicate [2]. However, AI does create some substitution risk: large language models could theoretically aggregate public real estate data and reduce the premium on CoStar's proprietary datasets. Residential real estate search (Homes.com, Zillow) faces AI disruption as conversational AI could disrupt traditional marketplace-based search models. CoStar's antitrust litigation also highlights that its competitive moat relies partly on data exclusivity that regulators may force open [3]. AI Opportunity (1/1): CoStar is actively generating value from AI through the Homes.com AI-powered search application (launched February 2026), which drove a 119% increase in organic traffic [1]. Matterport's 3D digital twin technology adds AI-driven spatial computing capabilities to the platform.

infinite_game.summary: CoStar Group under founder-CEO Andy Florance is a strong infinite game player. Florance has led the company for nearly 40 years with a consistent vision of digitizing and democratizing real estate information, expanding from commercial data to multifamily listings to residential to 3D digital twins. The willingness to invest billions in Homes.com (compressing near-term profitability for long-term market position) and the Matterport acquisition demonstrate courage to sacrifice today's earnings for tomorrow's platform dominance.

just_cause.analysis: Just Cause (2/3): Florance's founding vision of digitizing real estate information to create market transparency has been genuinely distinctive and consistently executed since 1986 [2]. Strategic decisions — entering multifamily with Apartments.com, challenging Zillow with Homes.com, acquiring Matterport for 3D technology — clearly trace back to this expanding vision of real estate information democratization. The cause has guided the company through multiple market cycles. However, it hasn't yet been validated through a true existential crisis or leadership transition.

trusting_teams.analysis: Trusting Teams (1/2): Florance's nearly 40-year tenure as founder-CEO provides exceptional leadership continuity, and CoStar's engineering-driven culture has attracted over 2,000 technology professionals [2]. The company has been recognized by Forbes and Fortune as innovative. However, the antitrust litigation alleges aggressive competitive tactics (exclusionary contracts, technological barriers) that could indicate an internally competitive culture [3]. No specific employee tenure or turnover data available.

worthy_rivals.analysis: Worthy Rivals (1/1): CoStar's decision to launch Homes.com in direct competition with Zillow — acknowledging the residential market leader's strengths while building a differentiated product through AI-powered search — reflects an infinite mindset toward competition [1][2]. Rather than accepting Zillow's dominance, Florance invested billions to build a better product. Management commentary frames the competitive challenge as an opportunity to innovate rather than a war to win.

existential_flexibility.analysis: Existential Flexibility (2/2): CoStar has fundamentally reinvented itself multiple times — from commercial real estate data compiler to online marketplace platform (Apartments.com acquisition 2014), to residential real estate challenger (Homes.com launch), to spatial computing company (Matterport acquisition) [1][2]. Each pivot expanded the addressable market by orders of magnitude and was driven by the Just Cause of democratizing real estate information, not by desperation. The decision to invest billions in Homes.com while compressing near-term profitability mirrors Sinek's existential flexibility archetype.

courage_to_lead.analysis: Courage to Lead (2/2): Florance's decision to invest aggressively in Homes.com — compressing GAAP profitability to near-zero ($3M net income in Q1 2026) to build long-term residential market position against the entrenched Zillow — is a textbook example of sacrificing near-term results for long-term capability [1]. The $1.6B Matterport acquisition bet on 3D digital twin technology for real estate, and Florance personally invested $5M in shares in the past year despite the stock's decline from highs [5]. Pattern of consistently choosing bold, long-term investment over earnings optimization.

investment_thesis: CoStar Group is a founder-led platform company delivering 23% revenue growth to $897M in Q1 2026, with full-year guidance of $3.8B (17% growth), demonstrating accelerating momentum from Homes.com AI innovation and Matterport integration [1]. The AI-powered Homes.com search application drove 119% organic traffic growth in Q1, validating the billions invested in residential real estate. Adjusted EBITDA doubled YoY to $132M, signaling the inflection from investment phase to profitability. With virtually no debt (D/E 0.15%), $5.0M in founder-CEO insider buying, and a PEG ratio of 0.54, CoStar offers compelling growth at a reasonable valuation. Analyst consensus target of $48.85 implies 60% upside from the current $33.66 [4].
thesis_continued: The primary risks are competitive and legal. Multiple antitrust class-action lawsuits filed in 2026 allege CoStar monopolized commercial real estate data through exclusionary contracts and technological barriers — if successful, these could force CoStar to open its data moat, undermining the core competitive advantage [3]. GAAP profitability remains near-zero (0.73% profit margin, ROE 0.30%) as heavy Homes.com investment compresses earnings, requiring continued investor patience for the residential bet to pay off. Homes.com still trails Zillow significantly in residential market share, and the path to profitability in residential requires sustained double-digit traffic growth and agent monetization improvements. Forward P/E of 20.8x assumes significant earnings acceleration — any deceleration in Homes.com traction could compress the multiple.
key_catalysts: Homes.com AI-powered search application driving 119% organic traffic growth with potential to capture meaningful share from Zillow as agent monetization scales through 2026-2027
Adjusted EBITDA doubling YoY to $132M in Q1 2026 signals inflection toward profitability as investment-phase spending moderates and operating leverage kicks in
Matterport 3D digital twin technology integration across CoStar, Apartments.com, and Homes.com platforms creating differentiated immersive experience that competitors cannot easily replicate
key_risks: Multiple antitrust class-action lawsuits filed in April-June 2026 allege CoStar monopolized commercial real estate data through exclusionary contracts — adverse rulings could force open the proprietary data moat
GAAP profitability near zero (0.73% margin) as Homes.com investment compresses earnings, with residential real estate still trailing Zillow and requiring sustained heavy investment to gain share
Forward P/E of 20.8x and PEG of 0.54 assume continued 20%+ revenue growth and earnings inflection — any slowdown in Homes.com traffic or bookings growth could trigger significant multiple compression
